title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Why Does Reproducibility Matter?
Research is only as reliable as its ability to be reproduced.

Science depends on independent verification of results
Your future self needs to re-run analyses months later
Collaborators need to understand and execute your workflow
Reviewers & journals increasingly require reproducible code and data



Reproducibility = same data + same code → same results

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
The Current State of Affairs
Most computational research is born in notebooks but…

Peer-review workflows don’t natively support notebooks as outputs
Complex projects involve a lot of manual work to reach submission stage
During this process, reproducibility is often lost
Final publications rarely capture all computations


It starts reproducible, then ends in a PDF or Word document. 😬

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
section: What Does a Reproducible Workflow Look Like?
text:
What Does a Reproducible Workflow Look Like?
Raw Data  →  Analysis Code  →  Results  →  Report
    ↑                ↑             ↑            ↑
 Versioned       Versioned      Auto-          Auto-
 (Git/DVC)       (Git)         generated      generated

The goal: One command to go from raw data → finished report, every time.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Quarto: Documents as Code
Quarto is an open-source scientific publishing system that:


Inputs

.qmd files (Quarto Markdown)
Jupyter notebooks (.ipynb)
R, Python, Julia, Observable JS


Outputs

HTML reports & websites
PDF / LaTeX
MS Word / PowerPoint
Presentations
Scientific manuscripts



Code, narrative, and output live in the same file — no copy-paste.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
The Full Complexity Spectrum
Not all projects are the same — Quarto scales with you:








Complexity
Setup
Use case




Simplest
Single .qmd
Blog post, homework, quick report


Simple
.qmd + journal extension
Journal article with formatting


Complex
Quarto project
Multi-file analysis, websites, books


Full
type: manuscript
End-to-end reproducible publication

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Quarto Manuscript Projects
Quarto 1.4+ introduced a new project type — type: manuscript:

Produce a manuscript in multiple formats simultaneously (HTML, PDF, Word, JATS)
Publish computations from notebooks alongside the paper
Readers can view or interact with your code in a virtual environment (Binder)
Treat the notebook as a primary scientific artifact, not an afterthought

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Anatomy of a .qmd File
---
title: "My Analysis"
format: html
execute:
  echo: false
---

## Results

Here is a summary of the data:

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
penguins |> 
  group_by(species) |> 
  summarize(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE))
```

One source → many outputs: HTML, PDF, Word, slides — all from the same .qmd.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
The Example Project Structure
When using the embed workflow, your project has two distinct layers:
my-manuscript/
├── _quarto.yml          # project config
├── index.qmd            # the manuscript (narrative)
├── references.bib
└── notebooks/
    └── analysis.qmd     # the computation (figures, tables)

index.qmd contains your writing.
notebooks/analysis.qmd contains your code.
They stay in sync automatically.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Step 1: Conduct Your Analysis
In notebooks/analysis.qmd, every figure gets a label:
#| label: fig-iris-sepal
#| fig-cap: "Sepal length vs. width across three iris species"

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#13294B", "#4B9CD3", "#99C4E2")) +
  theme_minimal()

The #| label: fig-* cell option is the hook — it’s how Quarto knows what to embed.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Step 3: Configure Your Manuscript
---
title: "Exploring Classic R Datasets"
author:
  - name: Russell Blessing
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0000
    corresponding: true
    email: rblessing@unc.edu
    affiliations:
      - UNC Chapel Hill
date: today
abstract: |
  A reproducible analysis demonstrating the embed workflow...
keywords: [reproducible research, ggplot2, iris, mtcars]
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
  pdf: default
  docx: default
---

One YAML block → correctly formatted author info, abstract, and citations in every output format.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
section: What the Rendered Manuscript Looks Like
text:
What the Rendered Manuscript Looks Like


notebooks/analysis.qmd
Runs the code, produces figures with labeled chunks:

fig-iris-sepal — sepal scatter plot

fig-iris-petal — petal boxplot

fig-mtcars — weight vs. MPG


index.qmd
Pulls figures in via embed, adds narrative:

Cross-references with @fig-iris-sepal

Auto-numbered figures

Captions carried through

Citations from .bib file



quarto render → HTML + PDF + Word, all consistent, all from the same source.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
The Rendered Manuscript

  

Live link

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
section: The Payoff: Nothing Gets Out of Sync
text:
The Payoff: Nothing Gets Out of Sync

Update a figure in analysis.qmd → re-render → manuscript updates automatically
No re-exporting PNGs, no re-pasting into Word
execute: freeze: true in _quarto.yml caches results — fast re-renders without re-running all code
On Longleaf: run heavy computations as a SLURM job
Version control everything with Git → track changes to code, narrative, and results together

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
section: Git + GitHub: Version Control for Research
text:
Git + GitHub: Version Control for Research
A reproducible workflow needs version control:

Track every change to your code, data, and documents
Collaborate without emailing files back and forth
GitHub hosts your project and can auto-publish reports via GitHub Pages
Quarto projects have built-in publishing support, and Poisitron has Git integration out of the box


# Typical workflow
git add analysis.qmd
git commit -m "Add demographic analysis section"
git push origin main

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Longleaf: UNC’s HPC Cluster
Longleaf is UNC’s flagship computing cluster — free for all UNC researchers:


What it has

32,000+ CPU cores
130 NVIDIA L40S GPUs + A100s
124 TB memory
High-speed scratch storage
SLURM job scheduler


Good for

Large-scale data processing
Statistical computing
Machine learning / deep learning
Memory-intensive analyses
High-throughput batch jobs

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Accessing Longleaf
Option 1: Command line (SSH)
ssh <onyen>@longleaf.unc.edu
Option 2: Open OnDemand (browser-based)

Go to ondemand.rc.unc.edu
Launch RStudio or JupyterLab directly in your browser
No SSH required — great for interactive work


💡 RStudio in Open OnDemand is a great starting point for R users new to HPC.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
section: A Reproducible Research Workflow at UNC
text:
A Reproducible Research Workflow at UNC

Write your analysis in .qmd files in Positron, connected to Longleaf via SSH
Version everything with Git from day one
Push computationally intensive jobs to Longleaf via SLURM
Render to HTML, PDF, and Word on Longleaf with a single quarto render
Publish to GitHub Pages for sharing and transparency quarto publish gh-pages

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Benefits for Researchers


For you

Reproduce results months later
Onboard collaborators quickly
Catch errors early (code + text together)
Reuse analysis code across papers


For science

Transparent methods
Shareable computational environment
Journal-ready multiple formats
Reviewers can verify results

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:

Check the tutorial deck (coming this summer) for step-by-step setup

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Setting up Positron
Edit your ssh file on your local machine:
nano ~/.ssh/config
Copy/Paste replacing YOUR_ONYEN with your actual ONYEN:

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Setting up Positron (cont.)
Host longleaf
  HostName longleaf.unc.edu
  User YOUR_ONYEN
  ServerAliveInterval 60
  ServerAliveCountMax 3

Host b* c* g* t*
  User YOUR_ONYEN
  ProxyJump longleaf
  ServerAliveInterval 60
  ServerAliveCountMax 3

Host *.ll.unc.edu
  User YOUR_ONYEN
  ProxyJump longleaf
  ServerAliveInterval 60
  ServerAliveCountMax 3
Save and exit the file (Ctrl + O, Enter, Ctrl + X).

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Longleaf Integration
Login to longleaf from your terminal:
ssh longleaf
Create a SALLOC script for Positron sessions:
nano interactive
Then copy/paste the following:

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Longleaf Integration (cont.)
#!/bin/bash
# Adjust --cpus-per-task if using parallel processing
# Adjust --mem based on your needs

salloc \
  --job-name=positron \
  --cpus-per-task=1 \ 
  --mem=70GB \ 
  --nodes=1 \
  --ntasks=1 \
  --time=08:00:00 \
  --partition=interact
Save and exit the file (Ctrl + O, Enter, Ctrl + X).

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Longleaf Integration (cont.)
Submit the SLURM script to start an interactive session:
bash interactive
You will see something like this:
salloc: Pending job allocation 31348688
salloc: job 31348688 queued and waiting for resources
salloc: job 31348688 has been allocated resources
salloc: Granted job allocation 31348688
salloc: Nodes c0402 are ready for job
Copy the node name (e.g., c0402)

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Integrating Positron with Longleaf
In Positron, go to positron > settings > and type: kernel supervisor: run - Select the checkbox for “Run Kernels in a login shell”

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Integrating Positron with Longleaf (cont.)
Next type: cmd-shift-p or ctrl-shift-p to open the command palette and type: ssh

You will see something like this:

 Type: <<cluster id>>.ll.unc.edu and hit enter

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Create a Quarto Project
In Positron, go to New > New Folder from Git > and paste the URL of your GitHub repository Next, select New > New File > Quarto Project > Manuscript Project

This will create a new folder with the necessary files for a Quarto manuscript project
Use the index.qmd file as your main manuscript file and edit it with your content
Use the references.bib file to manage your bibliography and citations
Use the notebooks folder to create and organize your data analysis notebooks as qmd files
Click preview to see the rendered manuscript and make adjustments as needed

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Manucript Formatting & Publishing
Many journals now have their own Quarto template for manuscript formatting, found here. Follow their instructions for adding the template as an extension. Before publishing go to your GitHub repository.

Navigate to Settings > Pages
Under Branch, select gh-pages and click Save
Your manuscript will be published at https://<your-github-username>.github.io/<repository-name>/

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Manuscript Formatting & Publishing (cont.)
Back is Positron your yaml should look something like this:
project:
  type: manuscript
  resources:
    - bibliography.bib
    - notebooks/*.qmd
manuscript:
  article: index.qmd
format:
  html:
    comments:
      hypothesis: true
  docx: default
  jats: default
execute:
  freeze: true
bibliography: bibliography.bib

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Website Publishing and Sharing
Before publishing, make sure to commit and push your changes to GitHub.
To publish your manuscript as a website, run the following command in your terminal:
quarto publish gh-pages
Go to your GitHub repository and navigate to Settings > Pages and your live site should be listed.

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
section: What are we going to learn?
text:
What are we going to learn?

Git branches, GitHub issues, and GitHub pull requests
How to associate branched code changes with issues in Positron
How to integrate issues + branches into manuscript drafting

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Recap



name
function
location




Git
Reads changes to text files, keeps track of commits and branches
Local machine


GitHub
Stores Git repositories (code + history) with interfaces for issues and pull requests
Online


Positron
Integrated development environment (IDE) for R and Python that integrates with git and GitHub
Local machine, Longleaf Open On Demand

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Branches

Branches, tracked by Git, are an useful way to iterative and non-destructively develop code



git branch develop
git checkout develop
git commit ...
git commit ...
git checkout main
git merge develop

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Issues

Issues, tracked by GitHub, let you create todos with detailed logs, comments, and attributes
Each issue gets a number, e.g., #8
Specify assignees, labels, milestones (e.g., sets of issues), relationships (e.g., blocking issues)
Create sub-issues for complex steps
Use templates (example)
Example issue

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Pull requests (PRs)

Pull requests, tracked by Git and enhanced by GitHub, are proposals for merging branches
Valuable for collaboration: facilitates discussion and review of changes
Look similar to issues (numbered, comments, logs, attributes)
Can create rules on GitHub (e.g., requiring review)
Example PR

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Workflow

Create an issue to propose a modification
Create a branch off default (e.g., main)
Checkout and commit changes to branch
Publish branch to GitHub
Create a pull request
Review changes and merge pull request

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Associating branches with issues

Branch naming practice: username/issue# notation, e.g., jamespcollins/issue8
Associate branch and commits with issue by adding the issue # in commit messages

Issue numbers will also auto-link in GitHub issue or PR comments.
These associations appear in the issue/PR log

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Integrating with Positron
Positron can manage many of these steps for you via the “GitHub Pull Requests” extension

View of issues and PRs
Creating, naming, and checking out issue branches
Creating, reviewing, and merging pull requests

demo

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Integrating with Quarto manuscript writing

Use issue templates to remind you to update the manuscript (e.g., index.qmd)
Make manuscript updates in the same branch as your analysis code updates

demo

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Tips

Use Milestones to set deadlines for bundles of issues that accomplish a substantial step
Set repository rules to require PRs to change your default branch
Use more complex branching, especially for sub-issues, by branching off an existing main issue branch

Positron setting for how to create new branches githubIssues.workingBaseBranch

Issues can feed into Project views for visualization (e.g., Gantt) and custom attributes (example)
Remember, issues and PRs are subject to the repository visibility

title: Reproducible Research with Quarto
text:
Take-aways

Git branches, GitHub issues, and GitHub pull requests
Positron makes it easy to associate issues with branched code changes
You can use issues to break your analysis and drafting into small chunks and manage collaborative work
